Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol \((\bmod\,p)\).  
We are working inside the odd set
\[
O=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}.
\]
Then \(A=\{a\in O:\chi(a)=1\}\) and \(B=\{b\in O:\chi(b)=-1\}\).

Define
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
Using the indicator identities \(\mathbf{1}_{\chi=1}=\frac{1+\chi}{2}\) and \(\mathbf{1}_{\chi=-1}=\frac{1-\chi}{2}\) (on nonzero classes), we get
\[
S_A=\frac12\sum_{a\in O}\Bigl(1+\chi(a)\Bigr)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad
S_B=\frac12\sum_{a\in O}\Bigl(1-\chi(a)\Bigr)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}.
\]
Let
\[
T=\sum_{a\in O}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad D=\sum_{a\in O}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}.
\]
Then
\[
S_A=\frac{T+D}{2},\qquad S_B=\frac{T-D}{2},
\]
so
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(T+D)^2+(T-D)^2}{4}=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(T\)
Write \(a=2k+1\), \(k=0,1,\dots,\frac{p-3}{2}\). With \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{p}\) and \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\),
\[
T=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos((2k+1)\theta)=\frac{\sin(2n\theta)}{2\sin\theta}.
\]
Here \(2n\theta=(p-1)\frac{\pi}{p}=\pi-\frac{\pi}{p}\), so \(\sin(2n\theta)=\sin(\pi/p)\). Hence
\[
T=\frac{\sin(\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}=\frac12.
\]

### 2) Compute \(D^2\) via a Gauss sum modulo \(2p\)
Let \(\eta=e^{\pi i/p}=e^{2\pi i/(2p)}\). Then \(\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=\frac{\eta^a+\eta^{-a}}{2}\). Since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\), so \(\chi(-a)=\chi(a)\). Therefore
\[
2D=\sum_{a\in O}\chi(a)(\eta^a+\eta^{-a})
=\sum_{\substack{n\ (\mathrm{mod}\ 2p)\\ n\ \text{odd}}}\chi(n)\,\eta^n,
\]
where we view \(\chi(n)\) as \(\left(\frac{n}{p}\right)\) on odd \(n\) (and the even classes contribute nothing).

Define the Dirichlet character modulo \(2p\)
\[
\widetilde\chi(n)=\chi(n)\chi_0(n),
\]
where \(\chi_0\) is the principal character modulo \(2\) (so \(\chi_0(n)=1\) if \(n\) is odd, \(0\) if even). Then the last sum is exactly the Gauss sum
\[
2D=\tau(\widetilde\chi)=\sum_{n\ (\mathrm{mod}\ 2p)} \widetilde\chi(n)\,e^{2\pi i n/(2p)}.
\]

Because \(2\) and \(p\) are coprime and \(\widetilde\chi=\chi\cdot\chi_0\) factors into characters modulo \(p\) and \(2\), the Gauss sum factors:
\[
\tau(\widetilde\chi)=\chi(2)\,\tau(\chi)\,\tau(\chi_0).
\]
Now \(\tau(\chi)\) is the quadratic Gauss sum modulo \(p\), and since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\),
\[
\tau(\chi)^2=\chi(-1)p=p \quad\Rightarrow\quad |\tau(\chi)|=\sqrt{p}.
\]
Also
\[
\tau(\chi_0)=\sum_{b\ (\mathrm{mod}\ 2)}\chi_0(b)e^{2\pi i b/2}=0\cdot 1+1\cdot(-1)=-1,
\]
so \(|\tau(\chi_0)|=1\). Hence
\[
|2D|=|\tau(\widetilde\chi)|=|\chi(2)|\cdot|\tau(\chi)|\cdot|\tau(\chi_0)|
=\sqrt{p},
\]
and therefore
\[
D^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

### 3) Finish
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{p+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p=2017\).  
For odd \(a\in\{1,3,\dots,p-2\}\), we have \(a\in A \iff \chi(a)=1\) and \(a\in B \iff \chi(a)=-1\).

Define
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
Also set
\[
S_{\text{tot}}=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-2\\ a\ \text{odd}}}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=S_A+S_B,
\qquad
S_{\chi}=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-2\\ a\ \text{odd}}}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=S_A-S_B.
\]
Then
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_A+S_B)^2+(S_A-S_B)^2}{2}=\frac{S_{\text{tot}}^{\,2}+S_{\chi}^{\,2}}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(S_{\text{tot}}\)
Let \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{p}\) and \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\). Then the odd integers are \(2k+1\) for \(k=0,1,\dots,n-1\), so
\[
S_{\text{tot}}=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos\big((2k+1)\theta\big).
\]
Using the standard identity
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos\big((2k+1)\theta\big)=\frac{\sin(2n\theta)}{2\sin\theta},
\]
we get
\[
S_{\text{tot}}=\frac{\sin\left((p-1)\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin\left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}=\frac{1}{2}.
\]

### 2) Compute \(S_{\chi}^2\) via Gauss sums
Let \(\xi=e^{i\pi/p}\) and \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/p}=\xi^2\). Consider
\[
H=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\,\xi^a.
\]
Its real part is
\[
\Re(H)=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}.
\]
Pair \(a\) with \(p-a\). Since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(\chi(-1)=1\), hence \(\chi(p-a)=\chi(-a)=\chi(a)\), while
\[
\cos\frac{(p-a)\pi}{p}=\cos\left(\pi-\frac{a\pi}{p}\right)=-\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}.
\]
So terms cancel in pairs, giving \(\Re(H)=0\).

Now apply the “sign-flip” automorphism \(\xi\mapsto -\xi\):
\[
H'=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\,(-\xi)^a=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\,(-1)^a\xi^a.
\]
But \(-\xi = -e^{i\pi/p}=e^{i\pi}e^{i\pi/p}=e^{2\pi i\cdot \frac{p+1}{2p}}=\omega^{(p+1)/2}\), so
\[
H'=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\,\omega^{\frac{p+1}{2}a},
\]
which is the standard quadratic Gauss sum \(G(t)=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\omega^{ta}\) with \(t=\frac{p+1}{2}\). For prime \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\),
\[
G(t)=\chi(t)\,G(1),\qquad G(1)=\sqrt p,
\]
hence \(H'=\chi\!\left(\frac{p+1}{2}\right)\sqrt p\), a real number.

The odd-indexed partial sum in complex form is
\[
W:=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\ \text{odd}}}\chi(a)\,\xi^a.
\]
Since
\[
H=\sum_{\text{even }a}\chi(a)\xi^a+\sum_{\text{odd }a}\chi(a)\xi^a,\quad
H'=\sum_{\text{even }a}\chi(a)\xi^a-\sum_{\text{odd }a}\chi(a)\xi^a,
\]
we get
\[
W=\frac{H-H'}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
S_{\chi}=\sum_{\substack{a\ \text{odd}}}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=\Re(W)
=\frac{\Re(H)-\Re(H')}{2}=-\frac{H'}{2}.
\]
So
\[
S_{\chi}^2=\frac{(H')^2}{4}=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

### 3) Finish
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{S_{\text{tot}}^{\,2}+S_{\chi}^{\,2}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{2017+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p=2017\).  
Let \(O=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}\) (the odd residues in \(\{1,\dots,p-1\}\)).

### 1) Rewrite the two sums using \(\chi\)
For odd \(a\), we have
\[
\mathbf 1_{a\in A}=\frac{1+\chi(a)}2,\qquad \mathbf 1_{a\in B}=\frac{1-\chi(a)}2.
\]
Define
\[
S_0=\sum_{a\in O}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_\chi=\sum_{a\in O}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}.
\]
Then
\[
\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=\frac{S_0+S_\chi}{2},\qquad
\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}=\frac{S_0-S_\chi}{2}.
\]
Hence the desired expression is
\[
\left(\frac{S_0+S_\chi}{2}\right)^2+\left(\frac{S_0-S_\chi}{2}\right)^2
=\frac{S_0^2+S_\chi^2}{2}.
\]

### 2) Compute \(S_0\)
Write \(a=2k+1\), \(k=0,1,\dots,\frac{p-3}{2}\). Let \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), \(m=\frac{p-1}{2}\). Then
\[
S_0=\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\cos((2k+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2mx)}{2\sin x}.
\]
Here \(2mx=(p-1)\frac{\pi}{p}=\pi-\frac{\pi}{p}\), so \(\sin(2mx)=\sin(\pi/p)\). Therefore
\[
S_0=\frac{\sin(\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}=\frac12.
\]

### 3) Compute \(S_\chi\)
First consider the full sum over \(1\le a\le p-1\):
\[
C:=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}.
\]
Use the change of variable \(b\equiv 2a\pmod p\) (a permutation of \(\{1,\dots,p-1\}\)):
\[
\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\cos\frac{2\pi a}{p}
=\sum_{b=1}^{p-1}\chi\!\left(\frac{b}{2}\right)\cos\frac{\pi b}{p}
=\chi(2)\sum_{b=1}^{p-1}\chi(b)\cos\frac{\pi b}{p}
=\chi(2)\,C.
\]
But \(\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)e^{2\pi i a/p}\) is the quadratic Gauss sum, which for \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\) equals \(\sqrt p\) (real). Hence
\[
\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\cos\frac{2\pi a}{p}=\sqrt p,
\]
so \(\chi(2)\,C=\sqrt p\). For \(p\equiv 1\pmod 8\) we have \(\chi(2)=\left(\frac{2}{p}\right)=1\); indeed \(2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\). Thus
\[
C=\sqrt p.
\]

Now split \(C\) into odd and even parts:
\[
C=S_\chi+E,\qquad E:=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\ \text{even}}}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}
=\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(2k)\cos\frac{2\pi k}{p}
=\chi(2)\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2\pi k}{p}.
\]
Because \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(\chi(-1)=1\), and \(\cos\frac{2\pi(p-k)}{p}=\cos\frac{2\pi k}{p}\), the full sum doubles the half-sum:
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2\pi k}{p}
=2\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2\pi k}{p}.
\]
The left-hand side is \(\sqrt p\), so the half-sum is \(\frac{\sqrt p}{2}\). With \(\chi(2)=1\),
\[
E=\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
S_\chi=C-E=\sqrt p-\frac{\sqrt p}{2}=\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]

### 4) Finish
\[
\frac{S_0^2+S_\chi^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\left(\frac{\sqrt p}{2}\right)^2}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{2017+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\) (\(p=2017\)).  
We work with the odd set
\[
O=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}.
\]
Then \(A=\{a\in O:\chi(a)=1\}\) and \(B=\{b\in O:\chi(b)=-1\}\).

Define
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
Let
\[
S:=S_A+S_B=\sum_{a\in O}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad
D:=S_A-S_B=\sum_{a\in O}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}.
\]
Then
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_A+S_B)^2+(S_A-S_B)^2}{2}=\frac{S^2+D^2}{2}.
\]

---

### 1) Compute \(S=\sum_{a\in O}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}\)

Write \(a=2k+1\), \(k=0,1,\dots,\frac{p-3}{2}\), and let \(\eta=e^{i\pi/p}\). Then
\[
S=\Re\sum_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2}\eta^{2k+1}.
\]
This is a geometric series:
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2}\eta^{2k+1}
=\eta\cdot\frac{1-\eta^{p-1}}{1-\eta^2}.
\]
Since \(\eta^{p}=e^{i\pi}=-1\), we have \(\eta^{p-1}=\eta^{-1}(-1)=-\eta^{-1}\), hence
\[
1-\eta^{p-1}=1+\eta^{-1}.
\]
So
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2}\eta^{2k+1}=\eta\cdot\frac{1+\eta^{-1}}{1-\eta^2}=\frac{1+\eta}{1-\eta^2}.
\]
Using \(1-\eta^2=(1-\eta)(1+\eta)\), this becomes
\[
\frac{1+\eta}{(1-\eta)(1+\eta)}=\frac{1}{1-\eta}.
\]
Thus
\[
S=\Re\left(\frac{1}{1-\eta}\right).
\]
A standard computation gives \(\Re\left(\frac{1}{1-e^{i\theta}}\right)=\frac12\) for \(\theta\not\equiv 0\pmod{2\pi}\). Here \(\theta=\pi/p\), so
\[
S=\frac12.
\]

---

### 2) Compute \(D=\sum_{a\in O}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}\)

Let
\[
G:=\sum_{a\in O}\chi(a)\eta^{a}\qquad(\eta=e^{i\pi/p}).
\]
Then \(D=\Re(G)\).

Now consider the (imprimitive) character modulo \(2p\):
\[
\psi(n)=
\begin{cases}
\chi(n) & n\ \text{odd},\\
0& n\ \text{even}.
\end{cases}
\]
Its Gauss sum is
\[
\tau:=\sum_{n\bmod 2p}\psi(n)e^{2\pi i n/(2p)}=\sum_{\substack{n\bmod 2p\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\eta^n.
\]
This \(\psi\) is induced from the primitive \(\chi\) modulo \(p\), and a standard induced-Gauss-sum formula gives
\[
\tau=\mu(2)\chi(2)\,\tau_p(\chi)=-\chi(2)\,\tau_p(\chi).
\]
Since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), the classical quadratic Gauss sum satisfies \(\tau_p(\chi)=\sqrt p\). Hence
\[
\tau=-\chi(2)\sqrt p.
\]

Next split \(\tau\) into odd residues \(1,3,\dots,p-2\) (which is exactly \(O\)) and the odd residues \(p+2, p+4,\dots,2p-1\). For \(j=1,\dots,\frac{p-1}{2}\),
\[
\chi(p+2j)=\chi(2j)=\chi(2)\chi(j),\qquad
\eta^{p+2j}=\eta^p\eta^{2j}=(-1)\cdot e^{2\pi i j/p}= -\zeta^j
\]
where \(\zeta=e^{2\pi i/p}\). Therefore
\[
\tau
=\sum_{a\in O}\chi(a)\eta^a\;+\;\sum_{j=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(p+2j)\eta^{p+2j}
=G-\chi(2)\sum_{j=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(j)\zeta^j.
\]
Let
\[
H:=\sum_{j=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(j)\zeta^j.
\]
Then \(G=\tau+\chi(2)H\), so
\[
D=\Re(G)=\Re(\tau)+\chi(2)\Re(H).
\]

But the full Gauss sum is
\[
\sum_{j=1}^{p-1}\chi(j)\zeta^j=\sqrt p\in\mathbb{R}.
\]
Also \(\chi(-j)=\chi(j)\) since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), so
\[
\sqrt p=\sum_{j=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(j)(\zeta^j+\zeta^{-j})=2\Re(H),
\]
hence \(\Re(H)=\frac{\sqrt p}{2}\). Therefore
\[
D=\left(-\chi(2)\sqrt p\right)+\chi(2)\cdot\frac{\sqrt p}{2}=-\chi(2)\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]
So
\[
D^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

---

### 3) Finish

\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{S^2+D^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{2017+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol modulo the odd prime \(p=2017\).  
Let \(O=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}\) be the odd residues.

Define
\[
S_A=\sum_{\substack{a\in O\\ \chi(a)=1}}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad
S_B=\sum_{\substack{b\in O\\ \chi(b)=-1}}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
Also set
\[
S:=\sum_{k\in O}\cos\frac{k\pi}{p},\qquad
D:=\sum_{k\in O}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}.
\]
Then
\[
S_A=\frac{S+D}{2},\qquad S_B=\frac{S-D}{2},
\]
so
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S+D)^2+(S-D)^2}{4}=\frac{S^2+D^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(S\)
Write \(\zeta=e^{i\pi/p}\). Then
\[
S=\Re\sum_{j=0}^{\frac{p-3}{2}}\zeta^{2j+1}
=\Re\left(\zeta\cdot \frac{1-(\zeta^2)^{\frac{p-1}{2}}}{1-\zeta^2}\right).
\]
Since \((\zeta^2)^{(p-1)/2}=e^{i\pi(p-1)/p}=-e^{-i\pi/p}=-\zeta^{-1}\), the numerator becomes \(1-(-\zeta^{-1})=1+\zeta^{-1}\), hence
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{\frac{p-3}{2}}\zeta^{2j+1}=\frac{\zeta+1}{1-\zeta^2}=\frac{1}{1-\zeta}.
\]
Now \(1-\zeta=1-e^{i\theta}=-2i\,e^{i\theta/2}\sin(\theta/2)\) with \(\theta=\pi/p\), so
\[
\frac{1}{1-\zeta}=\frac{i e^{-i\theta/2}}{2\sin(\theta/2)},
\]
whose real part is \(\dfrac{\sin(\theta/2)}{2\sin(\theta/2)}=\dfrac12\). Thus
\[
S=\frac12.
\]

### 2) Compute \(D\) via Gauss sums
Let \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/p}\), so \(\zeta^2=\omega\). Consider
\[
T:=\sum_{k\in O}\chi(k)\zeta^k \quad\Rightarrow\quad D=\Re(T).
\]
For odd \(k\), \(m=\frac{k+p}{2}\) is an integer in \(\{(p+1)/2,\dots,p-1\}\), and
\[
\zeta^k=e^{i\pi k/p}=-e^{2\pi i m/p}=-\omega^m.
\]
Also \(k\equiv 2m\pmod p\), so \(\chi(k)=\chi(2m)=\chi(2)\chi(m)\). Therefore
\[
T=-\chi(2)\sum_{m=\frac{p+1}{2}}^{p-1}\chi(m)\omega^m.
\]
Let
\[
H:=\sum_{m=1}^{\frac{p-1}{2}}\chi(m)\omega^m,\qquad
U:=\sum_{m=\frac{p+1}{2}}^{p-1}\chi(m)\omega^m.
\]
Using \(m=p-t\) (with \(t=1,\dots,(p-1)/2\)) and \(\chi(-1)=1\) since \(p\equiv1\pmod4\),
\[
U=\sum_{t=1}^{\frac{p-1}{2}}\chi(p-t)\omega^{p-t}
=\sum_{t=1}^{\frac{p-1}{2}}\chi(-t)\omega^{-t}
=\sum_{t=1}^{\frac{p-1}{2}}\chi(t)\omega^{-t}
=\overline{H}.
\]
The quadratic Gauss sum is
\[
G:=\sum_{m=1}^{p-1}\chi(m)\omega^m=H+U=H+\overline{H}=2\Re(H).
\]
For \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(G=\sqrt{p}\) (a positive real number), hence
\[
\Re(H)=\frac{\sqrt{p}}{2},\qquad \Re(U)=\Re(\overline{H})=\frac{\sqrt{p}}{2}.
\]
So
\[
D=\Re(T)=-\chi(2)\Re(U)=-\chi(2)\frac{\sqrt{p}}{2}.
\]
Here \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\), so \(\chi(2)=\left(\frac{2}{p}\right)=1\), giving
\[
D=-\frac{\sqrt{2017}}{2}.
\]

### 3) Final value
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{S^2+D^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\left(\frac{\sqrt{2017}}{2}\right)^2}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{2018}{8}
=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(p=2017\) and let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol.  
Let
\[
T=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-2\\ k\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{k\pi}{p},\qquad 
D=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-2\\ k\text{ odd}}}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}.
\]
Then numbers in \(A\) have \(\chi(k)=1\) and in \(B\) have \(\chi(k)=-1\), so
\[
\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=\frac{T+D}{2},\qquad 
\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}=\frac{T-D}{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
\left(\frac{T+D}{2}\right)^2+\left(\frac{T-D}{2}\right)^2=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(T\)
Write \(k=2j+1\), \(j=0,1,\dots,\frac{p-3}{2}\). With \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{p}\) and \(m=\frac{p-1}{2}\),
\[
T=\sum_{j=0}^{m-1}\cos((2j+1)\theta)=\frac{\sin(2m\theta)}{2\sin\theta}
=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}=\frac12.
\]

### 2) Compute \(D\)
Let \(t=\frac{p+1}{2}\). Then
\[
\cos\left(\frac{2\pi tk}{p}\right)=\cos\left(\pi k+\frac{\pi k}{p}\right)=(-1)^k\cos\left(\frac{\pi k}{p}\right),
\]
so for odd \(k\),
\[
\cos\left(\frac{\pi k}{p}\right)=-\cos\left(\frac{2\pi tk}{p}\right).
\]
Therefore
\[
D=-\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-1\\ k\text{ odd}}}\chi(k)\cos\left(\frac{2\pi tk}{p}\right).
\]
Let
\[
S=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\cos\left(\frac{2\pi tk}{p}\right),\quad
E=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-1\\ k\text{ even}}}\chi(k)\cos\left(\frac{2\pi tk}{p}\right),\quad
O=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-1\\ k\text{ odd}}}\chi(k)\cos\left(\frac{2\pi tk}{p}\right).
\]
Then \(S=E+O\) and \(D=-O\).

**Evaluate \(S\):** With \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/p}\),
\[
S=\Re\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\omega^{tk}.
\]
The quadratic Gauss sum satisfies \(\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\omega^{ak}=\chi(a)\tau\), and since \(p\equiv1\pmod 4\), \(\tau=\sqrt p\) (real). Also \(t\equiv 2^{-1}\pmod p\), so \(\chi(t)=\chi(2)\). Since \(p=2017\equiv1\pmod 8\), \(\chi(2)=1\). Thus
\[
S=\chi(t)\sqrt p=\sqrt p.
\]

**Evaluate \(E\):** Write even \(k=2m\), \(m=1,\dots,\frac{p-1}{2}\). Then
\[
E=\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(2m)\cos\left(\frac{4\pi tm}{p}\right).
\]
Here \(\chi(2m)=\chi(2)\chi(m)=\chi(m)\) (since \(\chi(2)=1\)), and \(4t=2(p+1)\equiv2\pmod p\), so
\[
E=\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\cos\left(\frac{4\pi m}{p}\right).
\]
Now consider the full sum
\[
S_2=\sum_{m=1}^{p-1}\chi(m)\cos\left(\frac{4\pi m}{p}\right)=\Re\sum_{m=1}^{p-1}\chi(m)\omega^{2m}=\chi(2)\sqrt p=\sqrt p.
\]
Because \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), \(\chi(-1)=1\), and pairing \(m\) with \(p-m\) shows \(S_2=2E\). Hence \(E=\frac{\sqrt p}{2}\).

Therefore
\[
O=S-E=\sqrt p-\frac{\sqrt p}{2}=\frac{\sqrt p}{2},
\qquad
D=-O=-\frac{\sqrt p}{2},
\qquad
D^2=\frac p4.
\]

### 3) Final value
\[
\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac p4}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac p4}{2}=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{2017+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(p=2017\) and let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol.

Define
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad 
S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
On the odd set \(\{1,3,\dots,p-2\}\), we have the decompositions
\[
S_A+S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-1\\ k\ \text{odd}}}\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}=:S_{\text{tot}},
\]
\[
S_A-S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-1\\ k\ \text{odd}}}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}=:S_{\text{diff}}.
\]
Then
\[
S_A=\frac{S_{\text{tot}}+S_{\text{diff}}}{2},\quad S_B=\frac{S_{\text{tot}}-S_{\text{diff}}}{2},
\]
so
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_{\text{tot}}+S_{\text{diff}})^2+(S_{\text{tot}}-S_{\text{diff}})^2}{4}
=\frac{S_{\text{tot}}^2+S_{\text{diff}}^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(S_{\text{tot}}\)
Let \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{p}\). Then
\[
S_{\text{tot}}=\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\cos\bigl((2j+1)\theta\bigr).
\]
Using the standard sum formula
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\cos(a+jd)=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{nd}{2}\right)\cos\left(a+\frac{(n-1)d}{2}\right)}{\sin\left(\frac d2\right)},
\]
with \(a=\theta\), \(d=2\theta\), we get
\[
S_{\text{tot}}=\frac{\sin(n\theta)\cos(n\theta)}{\sin\theta}
=\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{\sin(2n\theta)}{\sin\theta}.
\]
Here \(2n\theta=\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}=\pi-\frac{\pi}{p}\), so \(\sin(2n\theta)=\sin(\pi/p)\). Hence
\[
S_{\text{tot}}=\frac12.
\]

### 2) Compute \(S_{\text{diff}}\)
Write
\[
S_{\text{diff}}=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-1\\ k\ \text{odd}}}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}.
\]
Introduce the two full sums
\[
U:=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p},\qquad
V:=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)(-1)^k\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}.
\]
Since \(\frac{1-(-1)^k}{2}\) is the indicator of “\(k\) odd”, we have
\[
S_{\text{diff}}=\frac{U-V}{2}.
\]
Now split \(U\) into even and odd \(k\):
\[
U=U_{\text{even}}+U_{\text{odd}},\qquad V=U_{\text{even}}-U_{\text{odd}},
\]
so \(U_{\text{even}}=\frac{U+V}{2}\) and \(U_{\text{odd}}=\frac{U-V}{2}=S_{\text{diff}}\). In particular,
\[
S_{\text{diff}}=U_{\text{odd}}=U_{\text{even}}-V.
\]

**Compute \(U_{\text{even}}\).** For \(k=2j\),
\[
U_{\text{even}}=\sum_{j=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(2j)\cos\frac{2j\pi}{p}
=\chi(2)\sum_{j=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(j)\cos\frac{2j\pi}{p}.
\]
Because \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(\chi(-1)=1\), so pairing \(j\) and \(p-j\) gives
\[
\sum_{j=1}^{p-1}\chi(j)\cos\frac{2j\pi}{p}
=2\sum_{j=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(j)\cos\frac{2j\pi}{p}.
\]
But \(\sum_{j=1}^{p-1}\chi(j)e^{2\pi i j/p}= \sqrt{p}\) (the quadratic Gauss sum, real for \(p\equiv 1\!\!\pmod 4\)), so its real part is
\[
\sum_{j=1}^{p-1}\chi(j)\cos\frac{2j\pi}{p}=\sqrt{p}.
\]
Hence
\[
\sum_{j=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(j)\cos\frac{2j\pi}{p}=\frac{\sqrt{p}}{2},
\]
and therefore
\[
U_{\text{even}}=\chi(2)\cdot \frac{\sqrt{p}}{2}.
\]

**Compute \(V\).** Let \(\zeta=e^{2\pi i/p}\) and \(m=\frac{p+1}{2}\). Then
\[
(-1)^k e^{i\pi k/p} = e^{i\pi k}e^{i\pi k/p}=e^{2\pi i mk/p}=\zeta^{mk}.
\]
Thus
\[
V=\Re\left(\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\zeta^{mk}\right)=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\zeta^{mk}.
\]
By the Gauss sum twisting property,
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\zeta^{mk}=\chi(m)\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\zeta^k=\chi(m)\sqrt{p}.
\]
So \(V=\chi(m)\sqrt{p}\).

Therefore,
\[
S_{\text{diff}}=U_{\text{even}}-V=\sqrt{p}\left(\frac{\chi(2)}{2}-\chi(m)\right).
\]

Now evaluate the symbols for \(p=2017\):
- \(2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\), so \(\chi(2)=\left(\frac{2}{2017}\right)=1\).
- \(m=\frac{2017+1}{2}=1009\). Using reciprocity (note \(2017\equiv 1009\equiv 1\pmod 4\)):
\[
\left(\frac{1009}{2017}\right)=\left(\frac{2017\bmod 1009}{1009}\right)=\left(\frac{1008}{1009}\right)=\left(\frac{-1}{1009}\right)=(-1)^{(1009-1)/2}=1.
\]
So \(\chi(m)=1\).

Hence
\[
S_{\text{diff}}=\sqrt{p}\left(\frac12-1\right)=-\frac{\sqrt{p}}{2},
\qquad S_{\text{diff}}^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

### 3) Combine
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{S_{\text{tot}}^2+S_{\text{diff}}^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{2017+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\), and consider only the odd residues
\[
\mathcal O=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}.
\]
Define
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
On \(\mathcal O\), the indicator of “quadratic residue” is \(\frac{1+\chi(a)}2\), and of “nonresidue” is \(\frac{1-\chi(a)}2\). Hence if we set
\[
S_{\text{tot}}=\sum_{a\in\mathcal O}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad
S_{\chi}=\sum_{a\in\mathcal O}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},
\]
then
\[
S_A=\frac{S_{\text{tot}}+S_{\chi}}2,\qquad S_B=\frac{S_{\text{tot}}-S_{\chi}}2.
\]
Therefore
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_{\text{tot}}+S_{\chi})^2+(S_{\text{tot}}-S_{\chi})^2}{4}
=\frac{S_{\text{tot}}^2+S_{\chi}^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(S_{\text{tot}}\)
Let \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{p}\). Then
\[
S_{\text{tot}}=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos\bigl((2k+1)\theta\bigr)
=\frac{\sin(2n\theta)}{2\sin\theta}.
\]
Here \(2n\theta=\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}=\pi-\frac{\pi}{p}\), so \(\sin(2n\theta)=\sin(\pi/p)\). Thus
\[
S_{\text{tot}}=\frac{\sin(\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}=\frac12.
\]

### 2) Compute \(S_{\chi}\)
Let \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/p}\) and \(\zeta=e^{i\pi/p}\), so \(\zeta^2=\omega\).
Write cosine as a real part:
\[
S_{\chi}=\Re\left(\sum_{a\in\mathcal O}\chi(a)\zeta^a\right).
\]
Let
\[
T:=\sum_{a\in\mathcal O}\chi(a)\zeta^a.
\]
For odd \(a\), \((-1)^a=-1\), and note the identity
\[
\omega^{\frac{p+1}{2}a}=e^{2\pi i a\frac{p+1}{2p}}=e^{\pi i a}\,e^{\pi i a/p}=(-1)^a\zeta^a.
\]
Hence for odd \(a\), \(\zeta^a=-\omega^{\frac{p+1}{2}a}\), so
\[
T=-\sum_{a\in\mathcal O}\chi(a)\,\omega^{ma},\qquad m=\frac{p+1}{2}.
\]
Let the usual quadratic Gauss sum be
\[
g:=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\omega^a.
\]
For integer \(m\not\equiv0\pmod p\),
\[
\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\omega^{ma}=\chi(m)\,g.
\]
Split this sum into even and odd \(a\):
\[
\chi(m)g=\sum_{\substack{a=1\\ a\text{ even}}}^{p-1}\chi(a)\omega^{ma}+\sum_{a\in\mathcal O}\chi(a)\omega^{ma}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_{a\in\mathcal O}\chi(a)\omega^{ma}=\chi(m)g-\sum_{a\text{ even}}\chi(a)\omega^{ma}.
\]
If \(a=2t\), then \(\chi(a)=\chi(2)\chi(t)\) and \(\omega^{ma}=\omega^{2mt}=\omega^{(p+1)t}=\omega^t\). Thus
\[
\sum_{a\text{ even}}\chi(a)\omega^{ma}
=\chi(2)\sum_{t=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(t)\omega^t.
\]
Let \(H=\sum_{t=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(t)\omega^t\). Using \(\chi(p-t)=\chi(-t)=\chi(t)\) (since \(p\equiv1\pmod4\Rightarrow \chi(-1)=1\)), we get
\[
g=\sum_{t=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(t)\left(\omega^t+\omega^{-t}\right)=H+\overline{H}=2\Re(H),
\]
so \(\Re(H)=g/2\). Putting everything together:
\[
T=-\left(\chi(m)g-\chi(2)H\right)=\chi(2)H-\chi(m)g,
\]
hence
\[
S_{\chi}=\Re(T)=\chi(2)\Re(H)-\chi(m)g=\chi(2)\frac{g}{2}-\chi(m)g.
\]
For \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), \(g=\sqrt{p}\) (a real value). Also \(m=\frac{p+1}{2}\equiv 2^{-1}\pmod p\), so \(\chi(m)=\chi(2^{-1})=\chi(2)\). Therefore
\[
S_{\chi}=\sqrt{p}\left(\frac{\chi(2)}{2}-\chi(2)\right)=-\frac{\chi(2)\sqrt{p}}{2},
\]
and in particular
\[
S_{\chi}^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

### 3) Final value
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{S_{\text{tot}}^2+S_{\chi}^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{2017+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(p=2017\) (an odd prime). Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol.

Define sums over the odd residues \(1,3,\dots,p-2\):
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad 
S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
For an odd \(k\in\{1,3,\dots,p-2\}\), the indicator of “\(k\) is a quadratic residue” is \(\frac{1+\chi(k)}{2}\), and of “nonresidue” is \(\frac{1-\chi(k)}{2}\). Hence if we set
\[
S=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-2\\ k\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{k\pi}{p},\qquad 
T=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-2\\ k\text{ odd}}}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p},
\]
then
\[
S_A=\frac{S+T}{2},\qquad S_B=\frac{S-T}{2}.
\]
Therefore,
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S+T)^2+(S-T)^2}{4}=\frac{S^2+T^2}{2}. \tag{1}
\]

### 1) Compute \(S\)
Write \(k=2j+1\), \(j=0,1,\dots,\frac{p-3}{2}\). Then
\[
S=\sum_{j=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2j+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
This is a standard cosine arithmetic progression sum:
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{m-1}\cos(a+jd)=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{md}{2}\right)\cos\left(a+\frac{(m-1)d}{2}\right)}{\sin\left(\frac d2\right)}.
\]
Here \(m=\frac{p-1}{2}\), \(a=\frac{\pi}{p}\), \(d=\frac{2\pi}{p}\). Thus
\[
S=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{2p}\right)\cos\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{2p}\right)}{\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\frac12\sin(\pi/p)}{\sin(\pi/p)}=\frac12. \tag{2}
\]

### 2) Compute \(T\) via a Gauss sum
Let \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/p}\). Consider the quadratic Gauss sum
\[
g=\sum_{x=0}^{p-1}\chi(x)\omega^{x}.
\]
Since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), we have \(g=\sqrt{p}\) (a real number), and for any \(r\not\equiv 0\pmod p\),
\[
\sum_{x=0}^{p-1}\chi(x)\omega^{rx}=\chi(r)\,g. \tag{3}
\]

Now consider
\[
N:=\sum_{x=1}^{p-1}\chi(x)(-1)^{x+1}e^{i\pi x/p}.
\]
But \((-1)^x e^{i\pi x/p}=e^{i\pi x(1+1/p)}=e^{2\pi i x\cdot \frac{p+1}{2p}}=\omega^{rx}\) with \(r=\frac{p+1}{2}\). Hence
\[
N=-\sum_{x=1}^{p-1}\chi(x)\omega^{rx}=-\sum_{x=0}^{p-1}\chi(x)\omega^{rx}=-\chi(r)\,g. \tag{4}
\]

On the other hand, \(\Re(N)=2T\): indeed \((-1)^{x+1}\) is \(+1\) for odd \(x\) and \(-1\) for even \(x\), so \(\Re(N)\) is
\[
\sum_{x\text{ odd}}\chi(x)\cos\frac{x\pi}{p}-\sum_{x\text{ even}}\chi(x)\cos\frac{x\pi}{p},
\]
and since \(\sum_{x=1}^{p-1}\chi(x)\cos\frac{x\pi}{p}=0\) (pair \(x\) with \(p-x\), using \(\chi(-1)=1\) and \(\cos(\pi-\theta)=-\cos\theta\)), the even-sum equals \(-T\), making \(\Re(N)=T-(-T)=2T\).

Thus from (4),
\[
2T=\Re(N)=N=-\chi(r)\,g=-\chi(r)\sqrt{p},
\]
so
\[
T^2=\frac{p}{4}. \tag{5}
\]
(For \(p=2017\equiv1\pmod 8\), \(\chi(2)=1\) and indeed \(\chi(r)=\chi(2^{-1})=\chi(2)=1\), but only \(T^2\) is needed.)

### 3) Finish
Using (1), (2), and (5):
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{S^2+T^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{p+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol modulo the odd prime \(p=2017\).  
Let
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
Over the odd integers \(1,3,\dots,p-2\), we have \(\chi=1\) on \(A\) and \(\chi=-1\) on \(B\). Hence
\[
S_A+S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-2\\ a\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=:O,
\qquad
S_A-S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-2\\ a\text{ odd}}}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=:D.
\]
Therefore,
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_A+S_B)^2+(S_A-S_B)^2}{2}=\frac{O^2+D^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(O\)
Let \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\). Then
\[
O=\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\cos((2j+1)x).
\]
Using \(\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\cos((2j+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2nx)}{2\sin x}\), we get
\[
O=\frac{\sin(2n\cdot \pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin((p-1)\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{1}{2}.
\]

### 2) Compute \(D^2\) via Gauss sums
Let \(\zeta=e^{i\pi/p}\), so \(\zeta^2=\eta:=e^{2\pi i/p}\). Define the complex sum
\[
T_1:=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-2\\ a\text{ odd}}}\chi(a)\zeta^a,
\quad\text{so that}\quad
D=\Re(T_1).
\]

Now consider the “odd” Gauss-type sum over all odd \(n\) modulo \(2p\) except \(n=p\):
\[
\tau:=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le 2p-1\\ n\text{ odd},\,n\ne p}}\chi(n\bmod p)\,\zeta^n.
\]
Split it into the part \(n<p\) and the part \(n>p\). For \(n=p+2m\) with \(1\le m\le \frac{p-1}{2}\), we have \(n\bmod p=2m\), \(\zeta^{p+2m}=\zeta^p\zeta^{2m}=-\eta^m\), and \(\chi(2m)=\chi(2)\chi(m)\). Hence
\[
\tau
=\underbrace{\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-2\\ a\text{ odd}}}\chi(a)\zeta^a}_{T_1}
\;+\;\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(2m)\zeta^{p+2m}
= T_1-\chi(2)\underbrace{\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\eta^m}_{V}.
\]
So
\[
\tau=T_1-\chi(2)V \quad\Rightarrow\quad T_1=\tau+\chi(2)V.
\]

Next compute \(\tau\) in terms of the classical quadratic Gauss sum
\[
g:=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\eta^a.
\]
For each \(a\in\{1,2,\dots,p-1\}\), exactly one of \(a\) and \(a+p\) is odd; thus
\[
\tau=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\,(\pm \zeta^a),
\]
where the sign is \(+\) if \(a\) is odd and \(-\) if \(a\) is even, i.e. the factor is \((-1)^{a+1}\). Therefore
\[
\tau=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)(-1)^{a+1}\zeta^a
=-\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\zeta^{a(p+1)}
=-\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\eta^{a(p+1)/2}.
\]
Since \(\frac{p+1}{2}\equiv 2^{-1}\pmod p\), the standard Gauss sum twist property gives
\[
\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\eta^{ka}=\chi(k)\,g
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\tau=-\chi\!\left(\frac{p+1}{2}\right)g=-\chi(2)\,g,
\]
(using \(\chi(2^{-1})=\chi(2)\)). For \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\) (true for \(2017\)), the quadratic Gauss sum satisfies \(g=\sqrt p\) (real and positive). Thus
\[
\tau=-\chi(2)\sqrt p.
\]

Now we need \(\Re(V)\). Note
\[
g=\sum_{m=1}^{p-1}\chi(m)\eta^m
=\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\eta^m+\sum_{m=(p+1)/2}^{p-1}\chi(m)\eta^m.
\]
In the second sum, substitute \(m=p-t\). Since \(\chi(-t)=\chi(t)\) (because \(p\equiv 1\!\!\pmod 4\)), this becomes \(\overline{V}\). Hence \(g=V+\overline{V}=2\Re(V)\), so
\[
\Re(V)=\frac{g}{2}=\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]

Finally,
\[
T_1=\tau+\chi(2)V=\chi(2)\bigl(V-\sqrt p\bigr)
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
D=\Re(T_1)=\chi(2)\left(\Re(V)-\sqrt p\right)=\chi(2)\left(\frac{\sqrt p}{2}-\sqrt p\right)=-\chi(2)\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]
So
\[
D^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

### 3) Finish
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{O^2+D^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{p+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\) and set
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
All sums are over odd integers \(1,3,\dots,p-2\).

### 1) Reduce the required expression to two simpler sums
Let
\[
S=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-2\\ a\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=S_A+S_B,
\]
\[
T=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-2\\ a\text{ odd}}}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=S_A-S_B,
\]
since \(\chi(a)=1\) for \(a\in A\) and \(\chi(a)=-1\) for \(a\in B\).

Then
\[
S_A=\frac{S+T}{2},\quad S_B=\frac{S-T}{2}
\]
so
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S+T)^2+(S-T)^2}{4}=\frac{S^2+T^2}{2}.
\]

Thus we need \(S\) and \(T\).

---

### 2) Compute \(S\)
Write the odd numbers as \(a=2k+1\), \(k=0,1,\dots,\frac{p-3}{2}\). Let \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\). Then
\[
S=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos\left((2k+1)\frac{\pi}{p}\right).
\]
Using the standard identity
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos((2k+1)\theta)=\frac{\sin(2n\theta)}{2\sin\theta},
\]
with \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{p}\) and \(2n=p-1\), we get
\[
S=\frac{\sin\left((p-1)\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin\left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}=\frac12.
\]

So \(S^2=\frac14\).

---

### 3) Compute \(T^2\) via Gauss sums
Let
\[
\omega=e^{2\pi i/p},\qquad \zeta=e^{i\pi/p}\ \ (\text{so } \omega=\zeta^2).
\]
Consider the quadratic Gauss sum
\[
g=\sum_{x=1}^{p-1}\chi(x)\omega^x.
\]
For \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\) (here \(2017\equiv 1\pmod 4\)), we have
\[
g^2=\chi(-1)p=p.
\]

Now define
\[
P=\sum_{j=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(j)\omega^j.
\]
Using the fact \(\chi(-1)=1\) and the relation \(\omega^{(p-1)/2}=-\zeta^{-1}\), one checks that the second half of the Gauss sum is \(\overline{P}\), hence
\[
g=P+\overline{P}=2\Re(P)\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \Re(P)=\frac{g}{2}.
\]

Next, write \(T\) as the real part of a complex sum over odd \(a\):
\[
T=\Re\left(\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-2\\ a\text{ odd}}}\chi(a)\zeta^a\right).
\]
With \(a=2m+1\) this becomes
\[
\sum_{m=0}^{(p-3)/2}\chi(2m+1)\zeta^{2m+1}
=\zeta\sum_{m=0}^{(p-3)/2}\chi(2m+1)\omega^m.
\]
A completion-to-full-period computation (standard for such shifted character sums) gives
\[
\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-2\\ a\text{ odd}}}\chi(a)\zeta^a=\chi(2)\,(P-g).
\]
Taking real parts:
\[
T=\Re(\chi(2)(P-g))=\chi(2)(\Re(P)-g)=\chi(2)\left(\frac{g}{2}-g\right)=-\chi(2)\frac{g}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
T^2=\frac{g^2}{4}=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

### 4) Combine
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{S^2+T^2}{2}=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
For \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{p+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\).  
On the odd set \(\{1,3,\dots,p-2\}\), we have \(\chi(k)=1\) for \(k\in A\) and \(\chi(k)=-1\) for \(k\in B\).

Define
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
Set
\[
X=S_A+S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-2\\ k\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{k\pi}{p},\qquad
Y=S_A-S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-2\\ k\text{ odd}}}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}.
\]
Then
\[
S_A=\frac{X+Y}{2},\quad S_B=\frac{X-Y}{2}\implies
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{X^2+Y^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(X\)
Let \(m=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{p}\). Then
\[
X=\sumj_{j=0}^{m-1}\cos((2j+1)\theta).
\]
Using the standard cosine arithmetic progression sum,
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{m-1}\cos(a+jd)=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{md}{2}\right)\cos\left(a+\frac{(m-1)d}{2}\right)}{\sin\left(\frac d2\right)},
\]
with \(a=\theta\), \(d=2\theta\), we get
\[
X=\frac{\sin(m\theta)\cos(m\theta)}{\sin\theta}
=\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{\sin(2m\theta)}{\sin\theta}.
\]
But \(2m\theta=\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}=\pi-\theta\), so \(\sin(2m\theta)=\sin\theta\). Hence
\[
X=\frac12.
\]

### 2) Compute \(Y\) via Gauss sums (using \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\))
Here \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 4\). Let \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/p}\) and consider the quadratic Gauss sum
\[
\tau=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\omega^k.
\]
For \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(\tau=\sqrt p\) is real.

Let
\[
u=\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(k)\omega^k.
\]
Because \(\chi(-k)=\chi(k)\) when \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), we have
\[
\tau=\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(k)(\omega^k+\omega^{-k})=u+\overline{u}=2\Re(u),
\]
so \(\Re(u)=\tau/2=\sqrt p/2\).

Now connect \(Y\) to \(u\). Let \(t=\frac{p+1}{2}\), so \(t\equiv 2^{-1}\pmod p\). For odd \(k\),
\[
e^{i\pi k/p}=e^{2\pi i\,(k/2)/p}=-\,\omega^{tk},
\]
because \( \omega^{tk}=e^{2\pi i tk/p}=e^{\pi i k}e^{i\pi k/p}=(-1)^k e^{i\pi k/p}\) and \((-1)^k=-1\) for odd \(k\).

Thus the complex sum over odd \(k\) satisfies
\[
\sum_{\substack{k\ \text{odd}}}\chi(k)e^{i\pi k/p}
=-\sum_{\substack{k\ \text{odd}}}\chi(k)\omega^{tk}.
\]
Write the right side as (all \(k\)) minus (even \(k\)):
\[
\sum_{\substack{k\ \text{odd}}}\chi(k)\omega^{tk}
=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\omega^{tk}-\sum_{j=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(2j)\omega^{t(2j)}.
\]
The first sum equals \(\chi(t)\tau=\chi(2)\tau\). Also \(t(2j)\equiv j\pmod p\), so \(\omega^{t(2j)}=\omega^j\), and \(\chi(2j)=\chi(2)\chi(j)\). Hence the even part is \(\chi(2)u\). Therefore
\[
\sum_{\substack{k\ \text{odd}}}\chi(k)\omega^{tk}=\chi(2)(\tau-u),
\]
so
\[
\sum_{\substack{k\ \text{odd}}}\chi(k)e^{i\pi k/p}
=-\chi(2)(\tau-u)=\chi(2)(u-\tau)=-\chi(2)\overline{u}.
\]
Taking real parts gives
\[
Y=\sum_{\substack{k\ \text{odd}}}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}
=\Re\left(\sum_{\substack{k\ \text{odd}}}\chi(k)e^{i\pi k/p}\right)
=\Re\big(-\chi(2)\overline{u}\big)=-\chi(2)\Re(u).
\]
Thus \(|Y|=\Re(u)=\sqrt p/2\), so
\[
Y^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

### 3) Finish
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{X^2+Y^2}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{p+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(a)=\left(\frac ap\right)\) be the Legendre symbol (\(\chi(a)=1\) for quadratic residues, \(-1\) for nonresidues), and let
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
where \(A,B\) partition the odd set \(O=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}\).

Define
\[
C:=S_A+S_B=\sum_{a\in O}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad
D:=S_A-S_B=\sum_{a\in O}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}.
\]
Then
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_A+S_B)^2+(S_A-S_B)^2}{2}=\frac{C^2+D^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(C\)
Let \(\zeta=e^{i\pi/p}\) so that \(\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=\Re(\zeta^a)\). Then
\[
\sum_{a\in O}\zeta^a=\zeta+\zeta^3+\cdots+\zeta^{p-2}
=\zeta\frac{1-\zeta^{p-1}}{1-\zeta^2}.
\]
Since \(\zeta^p=-1\), we have \(\zeta^{p-1}=-\zeta^{-1}\), hence
\[
\sum_{a\in O}\zeta^a=\zeta\frac{1+\zeta^{-1}}{1-\zeta^2}=\frac{1+\zeta}{1-\zeta^2}
=\frac{1}{1-\zeta}.
\]
Write \(\zeta=e^{i\theta}\) with \(\theta=\pi/p\). Then
\[
\Re\!\left(\frac{1}{1-e^{i\theta}}\right)=\frac12,
\]
so \(C=\Re\left(\sum_{a\in O}\zeta^a\right)=\frac12\). Thus \(C^2=\frac14\).

### 2) Compute \(D^2\)
Let \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/p}\) and set \(k=\frac{p+1}{2}\). Then
\[
\omega^{ka}=e^{2\pi i ka/p}=e^{i\pi a}e^{i\pi a/p}=(-1)^a\zeta^a.
\]
For odd \(a\), \((-1)^a=-1\), hence \(\zeta^a=-\omega^{ka}\). Therefore
\[
D=\Re\!\left(\sum_{a\in O}\chi(a)\zeta^a\right)
=\Re\!\left(-\sum_{a\in O}\chi(a)\omega^{ka}\right)=-\Re(O_k),
\]
where \(O_k:=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\ \text{odd}}}\chi(a)\omega^{ka}\).

Now consider the full quadratic Gauss sum
\[
G(k):=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\omega^{ka}.
\]
For \(k\not\equiv 0\pmod p\), \(G(k)=\chi(k)G(1)\). Since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(G(1)=\sqrt p\) (a real number), hence
\[
G(k)=\chi(k)\sqrt p.
\]
Because \(k\equiv 2^{-1}\pmod p\), \(\chi(k)=\chi(2^{-1})=\chi(2)\). So \(G(k)=\chi(2)\sqrt p\) (real).

Split \(G(k)\) into even and odd \(a\):
\[
G(k)=E_k+O_k,\qquad
E_k:=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\ \text{even}}}\chi(a)\omega^{ka}.
\]
For \(a=2t\),
\[
E_k=\sum_{t=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(2t)\omega^{2kt}
=\chi(2)\sum_{t=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(t)\omega^{t},
\]
since \(2k\equiv 1\pmod p\). Let \(H:=\sum_{t=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(t)\omega^t\). Then \(E_k=\chi(2)H\).

But
\[
G(1)=\sum_{t=1}^{p-1}\chi(t)\omega^t = H+\overline{H}=2\Re(H)=\sqrt p,
\]
so \(\Re(H)=\frac{\sqrt p}{2}\). Hence
\[
\Re(E_k)=\chi(2)\Re(H)=\chi(2)\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]
Since \(G(k)=E_k+O_k\) and \(G(k)\) is real,
\[
\Re(O_k)=G(k)-\Re(E_k)=\chi(2)\sqrt p-\chi(2)\frac{\sqrt p}{2}
=\chi(2)\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
D=-\Re(O_k)=-\chi(2)\frac{\sqrt p}{2}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad D^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

### 3) Combine
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{C^2+D^2}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{2017+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(n)=\left(\frac{n}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p=2017\).  
We sum only over the odd integers \(1,3,5,\dots,p-2\).

Define
\[
C=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-2\\ n\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},\qquad
D=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-2\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
For odd \(n\), the indicator of being a quadratic residue is \(\frac{1+\chi(n)}2\), so
\[
\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=\frac{C+D}{2},\qquad
\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}=\frac{C-D}{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
\left(\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}\right)^2+\left(\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}\right)^2
=\frac{(C+D)^2+(C-D)^2}{4}=\frac{C^2+D^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(C\)
Let \(\zeta=e^{i\pi/p}\). Then
\[
\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-2\\ n\text{ odd}}}\zeta^n=\zeta+\zeta^3+\cdots+\zeta^{p-2}
=\zeta\left(1+\zeta^2+\cdots+(\zeta^2)^{\frac{p-3}{2}}\right).
\]
Since \(\zeta^2=e^{2\pi i/p}=\omega\) and \(\omega^{(p-1)/2}=e^{\pi i-\pi i/p}=-\zeta^{-1}\), this geometric sum equals
\[
\zeta\cdot \frac{1-(-\zeta^{-1})}{1-\zeta^2}=\frac{\zeta+1}{(1-\zeta)(1+\zeta)}=\frac{1}{1-\zeta}.
\]
Therefore
\[
C=\Re\left(\sum_{n\ \text{odd}}\zeta^n\right)=\Re\left(\frac{1}{1-e^{i\pi/p}}\right)=\frac12,
\]
since \(\Re\!\left(\frac{1}{1-e^{it}}\right)=\frac12\) for real \(t\not\equiv 0\pmod{2\pi}\).

So \(C^2=\frac14\).

### 2) Compute \(D^2\)
Let \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/p}\) (so \(\omega=\zeta^2\)). Consider the complex sum
\[
G_{\text{odd}}=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-2\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\zeta^n,
\quad\text{so}\quad D=\Re(G_{\text{odd}}).
\]
For odd \(n\), we use \(\zeta^n = -\omega^{(n+p)/2}\) because \(\omega^{p/2}=e^{\pi i}=-1\). Hence
\[
G_{\text{odd}}=-\sum_{n\ \text{odd}}\chi(n)\,\omega^{(n+p)/2}.
\]
Put \(m=\frac{n+p}{2}\). As \(n\) runs over \(1,3,\dots,p-2\), \(m\) runs over \(\frac{p+1}{2},\dots,p-1\). Also \(n=2m-p\equiv 2m\pmod p\), so \(\chi(n)=\chi(2m)=\chi(2)\chi(m)\). Thus
\[
G_{\text{odd}}=-\chi(2)\sum_{m=\frac{p+1}{2}}^{p-1}\chi(m)\omega^m.
\]
Let
\[
S_1=\sum_{m=1}^{\frac{p-1}{2}}\chi(m)\omega^m,\qquad
S_2=\sum_{m=\frac{p+1}{2}}^{p-1}\chi(m)\omega^m.
\]
Because \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(\chi(-1)=1\), and substituting \(m=p-k\) gives \(S_2=\overline{S_1}\). The full quadratic Gauss sum is
\[
g=\sum_{m=1}^{p-1}\chi(m)\omega^m=S_1+S_2=S_1+\overline{S_1}=2\Re(S_1),
\]
and for \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(g=\pm\sqrt{p}\) (real). Therefore \(\Re(S_2)=\Re(\overline{S_1})=\Re(S_1)=g/2=\pm\sqrt{p}/2\).

Now
\[
D=\Re(G_{\text{odd}})=-\chi(2)\Re(S_2)=\mp\,\chi(2)\frac{\sqrt{p}}{2},
\]
so in any case
\[
D^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]
For \(p=2017\), this gives \(D^2=\frac{2017}{4}\).

### 3) Final value
\[
\frac{C^2+D^2}{2}=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}=\frac{\frac{2018}{4}}{2}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol modulo \(p=2017\) (so \(\chi(a)=1\) for quadratic residues, \(\chi(a)=-1\) for nonresidues, and \(\chi(0)=0\)).

Define
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
Let \(O=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}\) be the set of odd integers in \(\{1,\dots,p-1\}\). Then \(A\cup B=O\) and \(A\cap B=\varnothing\), so
\[
S_A+S_B=\sum_{a\in O}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=:S_{\text{tot}},
\]
and also, since \(\chi(a)=\pm 1\) on \(O\),
\[
S_A-S_B=\sum_{a\in O}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=:S_{\text{diff}}.
\]
Hence
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_{\text{tot}})^2+(S_{\text{diff}})^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(S_{\text{tot}}\)
Write \(a=2k+1\), \(k=0,1,\dots,\frac{p-3}{2}\), and let \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{p}\). Then
\[
S_{\text{tot}}=\sum_{k=0}^{\frac{p-3}{2}}\cos\bigl((2k+1)\theta\bigr).
\]
Using the standard sum
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos\bigl((2k+1)\theta\bigr)=\frac{\sin(2n\theta)}{2\sin\theta},
\]
with \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\), we get
\[
S_{\text{tot}}=\frac{\sin\left((p-1)\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}=\frac{1}{2}.
\]

### 2) Compute \(S_{\text{diff}}\) via Gauss sums
Let \(\zeta=e^{i\pi/p}\), so \(\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=\Re(\zeta^a)\). Define
\[
O_\zeta:=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\text{ odd}}}\chi(a)\zeta^a,
\]
so that
\[
S_{\text{diff}}=\Re(O_\zeta).
\]
Also define the full sums (over all \(1\le a\le p-1\)):
\[
H:=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\zeta^a,\qquad
G_2:=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)(-\zeta)^a=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)(-1)^a\zeta^a.
\]
Split \(H\) and \(G_2\) into odd/even \(a\). If \(O_\zeta\) is the odd-part and \(E_\zeta\) the even-part, then
\[
H=O_\zeta+E_\zeta,\qquad G_2=-O_\zeta+E_\zeta \quad(\text{since }(-1)^a=-1\text{ for odd }a).
\]
Solving gives
\[
O_\zeta=\frac{H-G_2}{2}\quad\Rightarrow\quad S_{\text{diff}}=\Re(O_\zeta)=\frac{\Re(H)-\Re(G_2)}{2}.
\]

**(i) Show \(\Re(H)=0\).**  
Because \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\). Also \(\zeta^p=e^{i\pi}=-1\). Then
\[
\overline{H}=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\zeta^{-a}
=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(p-a)\zeta^{-(p-a)}
=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(-a)\zeta^{-p}\zeta^{a}
=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)(-1)\zeta^{a}=-H,
\]
so \(H\) is purely imaginary and \(\Re(H)=0\).

**(ii) Evaluate \(G_2\).**  
Note
\[
(-\zeta)^a=e^{i\pi a(1+1/p)}=e^{2\pi i a\cdot \frac{p+1}{2p}}
= e^{2\pi i a t/p},\quad \text{where } t=\frac{p+1}{2}.
\]
Thus
\[
G_2=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)e^{2\pi i a t/p}.
\]
For integer \(t\not\equiv 0\pmod p\), the quadratic Gauss sum satisfies
\[
\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)e^{2\pi i a t/p}=\chi(t)\,g,
\]
where \(g=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)e^{2\pi i a/p}\) is the (quadratic) Gauss sum. Since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(g=\sqrt p\) (a standard result). Also \(t\equiv 2^{-1}\pmod p\), so \(\chi(t)=\chi(2^{-1})=\chi(2)\). Therefore
\[
G_2=\chi(2)\sqrt p.
\]
Finally, \(\chi(2)=(-1)^{(p^2-1)/8}\). Since \(2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\), \(\chi(2)=1\), hence \(G_2=\sqrt p\) is real.

Putting (i) and (ii) into \(S_{\text{diff}}=\frac{\Re(H)-\Re(G_2)}{2}\) gives
\[
S_{\text{diff}}=-\frac{\sqrt p}{2}\quad\Rightarrow\quad S_{\text{diff}}^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

### 3) Finish
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_{\text{tot}})^2+(S_{\text{diff}})^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p=2017\).  
The odd numbers \(1,3,\dots,p-2\) are partitioned into
\[
A=\{a\ \text{odd}:\chi(a)=1\},\qquad B=\{b\ \text{odd}:\chi(b)=-1\}.
\]
Define
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
Let
\[
S:=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-2\\ a\ \text{odd}}}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad 
D:=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-2\\ a\ \text{odd}}}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}.
\]
Then \(S_A=\frac{S+D}{2}\) and \(S_B=\frac{S-D}{2}\), so
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S+D)^2+(S-D)^2}{4}=\frac{S^2+D^2}{2}.
\]
Thus we need \(S\) and \(D\).

---

### 1) Compute \(S\)
Let \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{p}\). Then
\[
S=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos\big((2k+1)\theta\big).
\]
Using the standard identity (from summing a geometric series of \(e^{i(2k+1)\theta}\)),
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} e^{i(2k+1)\theta}=\frac{e^{i2n\theta}-1}{2i\sin\theta},
\]
and here \(2n=p-1\) so \(e^{i2n\theta}=e^{i(p-1)\pi/p}=-e^{-i\pi/p}\). A short simplification gives the real part
\[
S=\frac12.
\]

---

### 2) Compute \(D\)

First consider the “all residues” cosine sum
\[
C:=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=\Re\left(\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\zeta^a\right),
\quad \text{where }\zeta=e^{i\pi/p}.
\]
Let \(S_1=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\zeta^a\). Then
\[
\overline{S_1}=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\zeta^{-a}
= -\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\zeta^{p-a}
= -\sum_{b=1}^{p-1}\chi(p-b)\zeta^{b}.
\]
Since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\), hence \(\chi(p-b)=\chi(-b)=\chi(b)\). Therefore
\[
\overline{S_1}=-S_1,
\]
so \(S_1\) is purely imaginary and thus \(C=\Re(S_1)=0\). Hence
\[
\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=0.
\]

Now split this sum into odd and even \(a\):
\[
0=C=\sum_{\substack{a=1\\ a\text{ odd}}}^{p-1}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}
+\sum_{\substack{a=1\\ a\text{ even}}}^{p-1}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}
= D + C_{\text{even}}.
\]
So \(D=-C_{\text{even}}\).

For \(C_{\text{even}}\), write \(a=2k\) with \(k=1,\dots,\frac{p-1}{2}\):
\[
C_{\text{even}}=\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(2k)\cos\frac{2k\pi}{p}
=\chi(2)\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2\pi k}{p}.
\]
Using the quadratic Gauss sum (for \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\)):
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)e^{2\pi i k/p}=\sqrt{p},
\]
taking real parts gives
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2\pi k}{p}=\sqrt{p}.
\]
Pairing \(k\) with \(p-k\) (noting \(\chi(p-k)=\chi(-k)=\chi(k)\) and cosine is equal) yields
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2\pi k}{p}=\frac{\sqrt{p}}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_{\text{even}}=\chi(2)\cdot \frac{\sqrt{p}}{2}.
\]
So
\[
D=-C_{\text{even}}=-\chi(2)\frac{\sqrt{p}}{2}.
\]
For \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\), \(\chi(2)=\left(\frac{2}{p}\right)=1\). Hence
\[
D^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

---

### 3) Final computation
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{S^2+D^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{p+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol modulo the odd prime \(p=2017\).  
Let
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
where \(A,B\) partition the odd set \(\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}\) into quadratic residues/nonresidues.

Define
\[
T=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\ \text{odd}}}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad 
D=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\ \text{odd}}}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}.
\]
Then, since \(\chi(a)=1\) on \(A\) and \(\chi(a)=-1\) on \(B\),
\[
S_A=\frac{T+D}{2},\qquad S_B=\frac{T-D}{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(T+D)^2+(T-D)^2}{4}=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(T\)
Write \(a=2k+1\), \(k=0,1,\dots,\frac{p-3}{2}\). Then
\[
T=\sum_{k=0}^{\frac{p-3}{2}}\cos\left(\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}\right).
\]
Using the standard sum formula \(\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos(\alpha+k\beta)=\frac{\sin(n\beta/2)\cos(\alpha+(n-1)\beta/2)}{\sin(\beta/2)}\) with  
\(n=\frac{p-1}{2},\ \alpha=\frac{\pi}{p},\ \beta=\frac{2\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
T=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{p-1}{2}\cdot\frac{\pi}{p}\right)\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{p}+\frac{p-3}{2}\cdot\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}{\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}
=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{2p}\right)\cos\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{2p}\right)}{\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}.
\]
Thus
\[
T=\frac{\tfrac12\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\tfrac12\sin\left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}{\sin(\pi/p)}=\frac12.
\]
So \(T^2=\frac14\).

### 2) Compute \(D^2\) via a Gauss sum
Let \(\zeta=e^{i\pi/p}\). Consider the induced character modulo \(2p\):
\[
\widetilde\chi(n)=
\begin{cases}
\chi(n\bmod p),& n\ \text{odd and } p\nmid n,\\
0,& \gcd(n,2p)>1.
\end{cases}
\]
Its Gauss sum is
\[
\tau=\sum_{n=1}^{2p}\widetilde\chi(n)e^{2\pi i n/(2p)}=\sum_{n=1}^{2p}\widetilde\chi(n)\zeta^n.
\]
Taking real parts,
\[
\Re(\tau)=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le 2p\\ n\ \text{odd},\ n\ne p}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Because \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\) (true for \(2017\)), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\), hence \(\chi(2p-n)=\chi(-n)=\chi(n)\), and \(\cos\!\left(\frac{(2p-n)\pi}{p}\right)=\cos\!\left(\frac{n\pi}{p}\right)\). Pairing \(n\) with \(2p-n\) shows
\[
\Re(\tau)=2\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\ \text{odd}}}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=2D.
\]
So \(D=\Re(\tau)/2\).

Now evaluate \(\tau\). Use the identity \(1_{\text{odd}}(n)=\frac{1-(-1)^n}{2}\):
\[
\tau=\sum_{n=1}^{2p}\chi(n\bmod p)\cdot \frac{1-(-1)^n}{2}\,\zeta^n
=\frac12\sum_{n=1}^{2p}\chi(n)\zeta^n-\frac12\sum_{n=1}^{2p}\chi(n)(-1)^n\zeta^n.
\]
The first sum is \(0\) because \(\zeta^{n+p}=-\zeta^n\) and \(\chi(n+p)=\chi(n)\), so terms cancel in pairs.

For the second sum, note \(( -1)^n\zeta^n=e^{i\pi n}e^{i\pi n/p}=e^{2\pi i n\cdot \frac{p+1}{2p}}\). Let \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/p}\). Then
\[
(-1)^n\zeta^n=\omega^{\frac{p+1}{2}n},
\]
and since \(\chi(n)\) is \(p\)-periodic,
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{2p}\chi(n)(-1)^n\zeta^n
=2\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\omega^{\frac{p+1}{2}a}.
\]
By the Gauss sum twist property,
\[
\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\omega^{ta}=\chi(t)\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\omega^{a}=\chi(t)\,\tau_p,
\]
where \(\tau_p=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\omega^a\) is the classical quadratic Gauss sum modulo \(p\). Thus
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{2p}\chi(n)(-1)^n\zeta^n = 2\chi\!\left(\frac{p+1}{2}\right)\tau_p.
\]
But \(\frac{p+1}{2}\equiv 2^{-1}\pmod p\), so \(\chi\!\left(\frac{p+1}{2}\right)=\chi(2^{-1})=\chi(2)\). Therefore
\[
\tau=-\chi(2)\tau_p.
\]
For \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(\tau_p^2=\chi(-1)p=p\), so \(\tau_p=\pm\sqrt p\) and hence \(\tau=\pm\sqrt p\) is real. Consequently,
\[
D=\frac{\Re(\tau)}{2}=\pm\frac{\sqrt p}{2}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad D^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

### 3) Finish
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{2017+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(k)=\left(\frac{k}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\) (quadratic character), and let
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
Over the odd set \(O=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}\), we have the indicator decompositions
\[
\mathbf{1}_{\chi(k)=1}=\frac{1+\chi(k)}{2},\qquad \mathbf{1}_{\chi(k)=-1}=\frac{1-\chi(k)}{2}.
\]
Hence, writing
\[
S_O=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-1\\ k\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{k\pi}{p},\qquad 
S_\chi=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-1\\ k\text{ odd}}}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p},
\]
we get
\[
S_A=\frac{S_O+S_\chi}{2},\qquad S_B=\frac{S_O-S_\chi}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_O+S_\chi)^2+(S_O-S_\chi)^2}{4}
=\frac{S_O^2+S_\chi^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(S_O\)
Let \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\) and \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\). Then
\[
S_O=\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\cos((2j+1)x)=\Re\left(\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}e^{i(2j+1)x}\right)
=\Re\left(e^{ix}\frac{1-e^{i2nx}}{1-e^{i2x}}\right).
\]
Here \(2nx=(p-1)\frac{\pi}{p}=\pi-\frac{\pi}{p}=\pi-x\), so \(e^{i2nx}=-e^{-ix}\). Thus the geometric sum simplifies and one obtains
\[
S_O=\frac12.
\]
(Indeed this identity holds for any odd prime \(p\).)

So \(S_O^2=\frac14\).

### 2) Compute \(S_\chi^2\) via a Gauss sum
Since \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\). Pairing \(k\) and \(p-k\) gives
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}=0
\]
because \(\chi(p-k)=\chi(-k)=\chi(k)\) while \(\cos\frac{(p-k)\pi}{p}=-\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}\).

Now use the odd-indicator \(\frac{1-(-1)^k}{2}\):
\[
2S_\chi=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\bigl(1-(-1)^k\bigr)\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}
= -\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)(-1)^k\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}.
\]
Let \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/p}\) and \(t=\frac{p+1}{2}\). Note
\[
(-1)^k\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}=\cos\left(k\pi+\frac{k\pi}{p}\right)=\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{p}\cdot tk\right)=\Re(\omega^{tk}).
\]
Hence
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)(-1)^k\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}=\Re\left(\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\omega^{tk}\right).
\]
The sum \(G(t)=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\omega^{tk}\) is a quadratic Gauss sum, and satisfies the standard relation
\[
G(t)=\chi(t)\,G(1)\qquad (t\not\equiv 0\!\!\pmod p).
\]
Also for \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(G(1)=\pm\sqrt{p}\) is real, so \(G(t)\) is real and
\[
\left(\Re(G(t))\right)^2 = (G(t))^2 = p.
\]
Therefore
\[
S_\chi=-\frac12\,\Re(G(t)) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad S_\chi^2=\frac14\,(\Re(G(t)))^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

### 3) Combine
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{S_O^2+S_\chi^2}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{p+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p=2017\).  
Let
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
Every odd \(m\in\{1,3,\dots,p-2\}\) lies in \(A\) iff \(\chi(m)=1\), and in \(B\) iff \(\chi(m)=-1\). Hence
\[
S_A=\sum_{\substack{1\le m\le p-2\\ m\text{ odd}}}\frac{1+\chi(m)}2\cos\frac{m\pi}{p},\qquad
S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le m\le p-2\\ m\text{ odd}}}\frac{1-\chi(m)}2\cos\frac{m\pi}{p}.
\]
So
\[
S_A+S_B=S_{\text{tot}}:=\sum_{\substack{1\le m\le p-2\\ m\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{m\pi}{p},
\quad
S_A-S_B=S_{\chi}:=\sum_{\substack{1\le m\le p-2\\ m\text{ odd}}}\chi(m)\cos\frac{m\pi}{p}.
\]
Therefore,
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_{\text{tot}}+S_\chi)^2+(S_{\text{tot}}-S_\chi)^2}{4}
=\frac{S_{\text{tot}}^2+S_\chi^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(S_{\text{tot}}\)
Write \(m=2k+1\), \(k=0,1,\dots,\frac{p-3}{2}\), and let \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{p}\), \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\). Then
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} e^{i(2k+1)\theta}
= e^{i\theta}\frac{1-e^{i2n\theta}}{1-e^{i2\theta}}.
\]
Here \(2n\theta=\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}=\pi-\frac{\pi}{p}\), so \(e^{i2n\theta}=-e^{-i\theta}\). Thus the numerator is \(1+e^{-i\theta}\), and taking real parts yields
\[
S_{\text{tot}}=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos((2k+1)\theta)=\frac12.
\]

### 2) Compute \(S_\chi\)
Let \(\zeta=e^{i\pi/p}\), \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/p}\). Note that \(\omega^{(p+1)/2}=e^{2\pi i (p+1)/(2p)}=e^{i\pi}e^{i\pi/p}=-\zeta\), hence
\[
(-1)^m\zeta^m = \omega^{m(p+1)/2}\quad\text{for all integers }m.
\]
Consider the “twisted” sum
\[
C_2:=\sum_{m=1}^{p-1} (-1)^m\chi(m)\zeta^m
=\sum_{m=1}^{p-1}\chi(m)\,\omega^{m(p+1)/2}.
\]
Since multiplication by \(\frac{p+1}{2}\equiv 2^{-1}\pmod p\) permutes \(\{1,2,\dots,p-1\}\), we can change variables \(t\equiv m(p+1)/2\pmod p\), i.e. \(m\equiv 2t\pmod p\), giving
\[
C_2=\sum_{t=1}^{p-1}\chi(2t)\omega^t=\chi(2)\sum_{t=1}^{p-1}\chi(t)\omega^t.
\]
The standard quadratic Gauss sum satisfies (since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\))
\[
\sum_{t=1}^{p-1}\chi(t)\omega^t=\sqrt{p},
\]
so
\[
C_2=\chi(2)\sqrt{p}\quad\text{(a real number)}.
\]

Now let
\[
C_1:=\sum_{m=1}^{p-1}\chi(m)\zeta^m.
\]
Its real part is
\[
\Re(C_1)=\sum_{m=1}^{p-1}\chi(m)\cos\frac{m\pi}{p}.
\]
Pair \(m\) with \(p-m\). Since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(\chi(-1)=1\) so \(\chi(p-m)=\chi(-m)=\chi(m)\), while
\(\cos\left(\frac{(p-m)\pi}{p}\right)=\cos\left(\pi-\frac{m\pi}{p}\right)=-\cos\left(\frac{m\pi}{p}\right)\).
Thus each pair cancels and \(\Re(C_1)=0\).

Let \(T=\sum_{\substack{1\le m\le p-2\\ m\text{ odd}}}\chi(m)\zeta^m\). Using \((-1)^m=1\) for even \(m\) and \(-1\) for odd \(m\),
\[
C_2=\sum_{m=1}^{p-1}(-1)^m\chi(m)\zeta^m
=\sum_{m=1}^{p-1}\chi(m)\zeta^m - 2\sum_{\substack{1\le m\le p-1\\ m\text{ odd}}}\chi(m)\zeta^m
= C_1-2T.
\]
Hence \(T=\frac{C_1-C_2}{2}\), and therefore
\[
S_\chi=\sum_{\substack{1\le m\le p-2\\ m\text{ odd}}}\chi(m)\cos\frac{m\pi}{p}
=\Re(T)=\frac{\Re(C_1)-\Re(C_2)}{2}=-\frac{C_2}{2}=-\frac{\chi(2)\sqrt{p}}{2}.
\]
So \(S_\chi^2=\dfrac{p}{4}\).

For \(p=2017\), \(2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\), so \(\chi(2)=\left(\frac{2}{p}\right)=1\) (sign irrelevant anyway), and indeed \(S_\chi^2=\frac{2017}{4}\).

### 3) Final value
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{S_{\text{tot}}^2+S_\chi^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac{2018}{4}}{2}
=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\).  
Let \(O=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}\) (the odd numbers in \(\{1,\dots,p-1\}\)).

Define
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
On \(O\), the indicator of “quadratic residue” is \(\frac{1+\chi(a)}2\), and “non-residue” is \(\frac{1-\chi(a)}2\). Hence
\[
S_A+S_B=\sum_{a\in O}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=:C_{\text{odd}},
\]
\[
S_A-S_B=\sum_{a\in O}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=:C_\chi.
\]
Therefore,
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_A+S_B)^2+(S_A-S_B)^2}{2}
=\frac{C_{\text{odd}}^2+C_\chi^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(C_{\text{odd}}\)
Write \(a=2k+1\), \(k=0,1,\dots,\frac{p-3}{2}\). Let \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\). Then
\[
C_{\text{odd}}=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos((2k+1)x).
\]
Using \(\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos((2k+1)x)=\dfrac{\sin(2nx)}{2\sin x}\),
\[
C_{\text{odd}}=\frac{\sin((p-1)\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}=\frac12.
\]

### 2) Compute \(C_\chi\)
Let \(\zeta=e^{i\pi/p}\). Consider
\[
T=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\zeta^a,\qquad
T_{\text{alt}}=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)(-1)^a\zeta^a.
\]
Filtering odd \(a\) gives
\[
\sum_{a\in O}\chi(a)\zeta^a=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\frac{1-(-1)^a}{2}\zeta^a
=\frac{T-T_{\text{alt}}}{2}.
\]
Taking real parts,
\[
C_\chi=\sum_{a\in O}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}
=\Re\left(\frac{T-T_{\text{alt}}}{2}\right)
=\frac{\Re(T)-\Re(T_{\text{alt}})}{2}.
\]

**(i) \(\Re(T)=0\).** Since \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\). Pair \(a\) with \(p-a\):
\[
\chi(p-a)=\chi(-a)=\chi(a),\quad \zeta^{p-a}=e^{i\pi(p-a)/p}=-\zeta^{-a}.
\]
So each pair contributes
\[
\chi(a)\zeta^a+\chi(p-a)\zeta^{p-a}=\chi(a)(\zeta^a-\zeta^{-a})=2i\chi(a)\sin\frac{a\pi}{p},
\]
purely imaginary. Hence \(\Re(T)=0\).

**(ii) \(T_{\text{alt}}=\sqrt p\).** Note \((-1)^a\zeta^a=e^{i\pi a}e^{i\pi a/p}=e^{2\pi i a\cdot \frac{p+1}{2}/p}\). Let \(k=\frac{p+1}{2}\). Then
\[
T_{\text{alt}}=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)e^{2\pi i ka/p}.
\]
This is a twisted quadratic Gauss sum, equal to \(\chi(k)G\), where
\[
G=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)e^{2\pi i a/p}.
\]
For \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(G=\sqrt p\). Also \(\chi(k)=\chi(2^{-1})=\chi(2)\). Since \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\), \(\chi(2)=1\). Thus \(T_{\text{alt}}=\sqrt p\), real.

Therefore,
\[
C_\chi=\frac{0-\sqrt p}{2}=-\frac{\sqrt p}{2}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
C_\chi^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

### 3) Assemble
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{C_{\text{odd}}^2+C_\chi^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{p+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol modulo the prime \(p=2017\).  
Define
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
Over the odd numbers \(1,3,\dots,p-2\), we have the partition by \(\chi\): \(\chi(k)=1\) for \(k\in A\) and \(\chi(k)=-1\) for \(k\in B\). Hence
\[
S:=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-2\\ k\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}=S_A+S_B,
\]
\[
D:=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-2\\ k\text{ odd}}}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}=S_A-S_B.
\]
Therefore
\[
S_A=\frac{S+D}{2},\quad S_B=\frac{S-D}{2}\implies S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{S^2+D^2}{2}.
\]
So we just need \(S\) and \(D\).

---

### 1) Compute \(S=\sum_{k\ \text{odd}}\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}\)

Let \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{p}\). With \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\),
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{n-1} e^{i(2j+1)\theta}=e^{i\theta}\frac{1-e^{i2n\theta}}{1-e^{i2\theta}}.
\]
Here \(2n=p-1\), so \(e^{i2n\theta}=e^{i(p-1)\pi/p}=e^{i(\pi-\theta)}=-e^{-i\theta}\). Thus
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{n-1} e^{i(2j+1)\theta}
= e^{i\theta}\frac{1+e^{-i\theta}}{1-e^{i2\theta}}
=\frac{1+e^{i\theta}}{(1-e^{i\theta})(1+e^{i\theta})}
=\frac{1}{1-e^{i\theta}}.
\]
Taking real parts,
\[
S=\Re\left(\frac{1}{1-e^{i\theta}}\right)=\frac12.
\]

---

### 2) Compute \(D=\sum_{k\ \text{odd}}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}\)

Let \(\omega=e^{i\pi/p}=e^{i\theta}\). Consider the complex odd-filtered sum
\[
C:=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-1\\ k\text{ odd}}}\chi(k)\omega^k,
\qquad\text{so } D=\Re(C).
\]
Use the parity filter \(\frac{1-(-1)^k}{2}\):
\[
C=\frac12\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\bigl(1-(-1)^k\bigr)\omega^k
=\frac12\left(\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\omega^k-\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)(-1)^k\omega^k\right).
\]
Let
\[
S_1=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\omega^k,\qquad
S_2=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)(-1)^k\omega^k.
\]
Then \(C=\frac12(S_1-S_2)\), hence
\[
D=\Re(C)=\frac12(\Re(S_1)-\Re(S_2)).
\]

**Claim:** \(\Re(S_1)=0\).  
Indeed, since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\). Then
\[
\overline{S_1}=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\omega^{-k}
=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(p-k)\omega^{-(p-k)}
=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\omega^{-p}\omega^{k}
=-\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\omega^k=-S_1,
\]
because \(\omega^p=e^{i\pi}=-1\). Thus \(S_1\) is purely imaginary, so \(\Re(S_1)=0\).

So \(D=-\frac12\Re(S_2)\). But \(S_2\) can be rewritten as a standard Gauss sum:
\[
(-1)^k\omega^k=e^{i\pi k}e^{i\pi k/p}=e^{i\pi (p+1)k/p}=e^{2\pi i \cdot \frac{p+1}{2}\cdot \frac{k}{p}}.
\]
Let \(m=\frac{p+1}{2}\) (an integer). Then
\[
S_2=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)e^{2\pi i mk/p}.
\]
By the standard quadratic Gauss sum identity,
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)e^{2\pi i mk/p}=\chi(m)\tau(\chi),
\]
where \(\tau(\chi)=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)e^{2\pi i k/p}\), and for \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\) one has \(\tau(\chi)=\sqrt{p}\) (real). Hence
\[
S_2=\chi(m)\sqrt{p}.
\]
Now \(\chi(m)=\left(\frac{(p+1)/2}{p}\right)=\left(\frac{2^{-1}}{p}\right)=\left(\frac{2}{p}\right)\).
Since \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\), \(\left(\frac{2}{p}\right)=1\). Therefore \(S_2=\sqrt{p}\), and it is real, so
\[
D=-\frac12 S_2=-\frac{\sqrt{p}}{2}\quad\Rightarrow\quad D^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

---

### 3) Finish
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{S^2+D^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{p+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\) (so \(\chi(a)=1\) for nonzero quadratic residues, \(\chi(a)=-1\) for nonresidues).

Let \(O=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}\) (the odd numbers in \(\{1,\dots,p-1\}\)). Then \(A\cup B=O\) and \(A\cap B=\varnothing\). Define
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
Set
\[
S:=S_A+S_B=\sum_{k\in O}\cos\frac{k\pi}{p},\qquad 
D:=S_A-S_B=\sum_{k\in O}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}.
\]
Then
\[
S_A=\frac{S+D}{2},\quad S_B=\frac{S-D}{2}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S+D)^2+(S-D)^2}{4}=\frac{S^2+D^2}{2}.
\]
So we just need \(S\) and \(D\).

---

## 1) Compute \(S=\sum_{k\in O}\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}\)

Let \(\zeta=e^{i\pi/p}\). Then \(\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}=\Re(\zeta^k)\), hence
\[
S=\Re\left(\sum_{k\in O}\zeta^k\right)=\Re(T),
\quad T:=\zeta+\zeta^3+\cdots+\zeta^{p-2}.
\]
This is a geometric series with ratio \(\zeta^2\):
\[
T=\zeta\frac{1-(\zeta^2)^{(p-1)/2}}{1-\zeta^2}.
\]
But \((\zeta^2)^{(p-1)/2}=e^{i\pi(p-1)/p}=-e^{-i\pi/p}=-\zeta^{-1}\). Therefore
\[
T=\zeta\frac{1-(-\zeta^{-1})}{1-\zeta^2}
=\zeta\frac{1+\zeta^{-1}}{(1-\zeta)(1+\zeta)}
=\frac{1}{1-\zeta}.
\]
Using the standard identity
\[
\frac{1}{1-e^{i\theta}}=\frac12+\frac{i}{2}\cot\frac{\theta}{2},
\]
we get \(\Re\!\left(\frac{1}{1-\zeta}\right)=\frac12\). Hence
\[
S=\frac12.
\]

---

## 2) Compute \(D=\sum_{k\in O}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}\)

Let
\[
U:=\sum_{k\in O}\chi(k)\zeta^k\quad\Rightarrow\quad D=\Re(U).
\]

Introduce the (imprimitive) Dirichlet character \(\psi\) modulo \(2p\) induced from \(\chi\):
\[
\psi(n)=\begin{cases}
\chi(n\bmod p),& n\ \text{odd},\\
0,& n\ \text{even}.
\end{cases}
\]
Its Gauss sum is
\[
\tau(\psi)=\sum_{n=1}^{2p}\psi(n)e^{2\pi i n/(2p)}=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le 2p\\ n\ \text{odd}}}\chi(n)\zeta^n.
\]
A standard fact for induced characters gives
\[
\tau(\psi)=\mu(2)\chi(2)\tau(\chi)=-\chi(2)\tau(\chi).
\]
Since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), the quadratic Gauss sum \(\tau(\chi)=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)e^{2\pi i a/p}\) equals \(\sqrt{p}\) (real). Thus
\[
\tau(\psi)=-\chi(2)\sqrt{p}\quad\text{(real)}.
\]

Now split \(\tau(\psi)\) into odds below \(p\) (which is exactly \(U\)) and odds above \(p\). For \(n=p+2t\) with \(t=1,\dots,\frac{p-1}{2}\),
\[
\chi(n)=\chi(2t)=\chi(2)\chi(t),\qquad \zeta^{p+2t}=\zeta^p\zeta^{2t}=(-1)\omega^t,
\]
where \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/p}=\zeta^2\). Hence
\[
\tau(\psi)=U-\chi(2)\sum_{t=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(t)\omega^t.
\]
Let \(H=\sum_{t=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(t)\omega^t\). The full Gauss sum is
\[
\tau(\chi)=\sum_{t=1}^{p-1}\chi(t)\omega^t=H+\overline{H}=2\Re(H)=\sqrt{p},
\]
so \(\Re(H)=\sqrt{p}/2\). Taking real parts in \(\tau(\psi)=U-\chi(2)H\) gives
\[
-\chi(2)\sqrt{p}=\Re(U)-\chi(2)\Re(H)=D-\chi(2)\frac{\sqrt{p}}{2},
\]
so
\[
D=-\chi(2)\frac{\sqrt{p}}{2}.
\]
For \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\), \(\chi(2)=\left(\frac{2}{p}\right)=1\). Therefore
\[
D=-\frac{\sqrt{2017}}{2}\quad\Rightarrow\quad D^2=\frac{2017}{4}.
\]

---

## 3) Finish
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{S^2+D^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\left(\frac{\sqrt{2017}}{2}\right)^2}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{2018/4}{2}
=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p=2017\).  
We are summing over the odd integers \(1,3,\dots,p-2\); for each such odd \(a\), \(\chi(a)=1\) iff \(a\in A\) and \(\chi(a)=-1\) iff \(a\in B\).

Define
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\quad
S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
Also define the “total” and “character” sums over odd integers:
\[
S_{\text{tot}}=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\ \text{odd}}}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad
S_\chi=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\ \text{odd}}}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}.
\]
Then
\[
S_A=\frac{S_{\text{tot}}+S_\chi}{2},\qquad S_B=\frac{S_{\text{tot}}-S_\chi}{2},
\]
so
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_{\text{tot}}+S_\chi)^2+(S_{\text{tot}}-S_\chi)^2}{4}
=\frac{S_{\text{tot}}^2+S_\chi^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(S_{\text{tot}}\)
There are \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) odd terms. Using the standard identity
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos\bigl((2k+1)x\bigr)=\frac{\sin(2nx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\) and \(2n=p-1\), we get
\[
S_{\text{tot}}=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}
=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}
=\frac{\sin\left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}
=\frac{1}{2}.
\]

### 2) Compute \(S_\chi\)
Let \(\omega=e^{i\pi/p}\), so \(\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=\Re(\omega^a)\). Also, the indicator of “\(a\) odd” is \(\frac{1-(-1)^a}{2}\). Hence
\[
S_\chi=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\frac{1-(-1)^a}{2}\,\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}
=\frac12\left(C-D\right),
\]
where
\[
C=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad
D=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)(-1)^a\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}.
\]

**(a) Show \(C=0\).**  
Let \(V=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\omega^a\). Then \(C=\Re(V)\). We compute \(\overline{V}\):
\[
\overline{V}=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\omega^{-a}.
\]
Substitute \(a\mapsto p-a\). Since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(\chi(-1)=1\), so \(\chi(p-a)=\chi(-a)=\chi(a)\). Also \(\omega^{- (p-a)}=\omega^{-p}\omega^{a}=(-1)\omega^{a}=-\omega^a\) because \(\omega^p=-1\). Therefore
\[
\overline{V}=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)(-\omega^a)=-V.
\]
So \(V\) is purely imaginary, hence \(\Re(V)=0\), i.e. \(C=0\).

**(b) Evaluate \(D\) using a Gauss sum.**  
Note that \((-1)^a\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=\Re\bigl(((-1)\omega)^a\bigr)=\Re\bigl((-\omega)^a\bigr)\). Thus
\[
D=\Re\left(\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)(-\omega)^a\right).
\]
Now \(-\omega=e^{i(\pi+\pi/p)}=e^{2\pi i\cdot \frac{p+1}{2p}}=\zeta^{m}\) where \(\zeta=e^{2\pi i/p}\) and \(m=\frac{p+1}{2}\). Hence
\[
\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)(-\omega)^a=\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\zeta^{ma}.
\]
This is the quadratic Gauss sum \(g(m)\), and for \(m\not\equiv 0\pmod p\),
\[
g(m)=\chi(m)\,g(1).
\]
For \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(g(1)=\sqrt{p}\) (a real number). Also \(\chi(m)=\chi\!\left(\frac{p+1}{2}\right)=\chi(2)\) because \(\frac{p+1}{2}\equiv 2^{-1}\pmod p\) and \(\chi(2^{-1})=\chi(2)\). Since \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\), \(\chi(2)=1\). Therefore
\[
\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)(-\omega)^a=\sqrt{p},
\]
so \(D=\Re(\sqrt{p})=\sqrt{p}\).

Putting (a) and (b) together:
\[
S_\chi=\frac12(C-D)=\frac12(0-\sqrt{p})=-\frac{\sqrt{p}}{2}.
\]
Thus \(S_\chi^2=\frac{p}{4}\).

### 3) Final value
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{S_{\text{tot}}^2+S_\chi^2}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{2017+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p=2017\).  
We work on the odd set \(O=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}\). Then
\[
\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=\sum_{\substack{x\in O\\ \chi(x)=1}}\cos\frac{x\pi}{p},\qquad
\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}=\sum_{\substack{x\in O\\ \chi(x)=-1}}\cos\frac{x\pi}{p}.
\]
Define
\[
S:=\sum_{x\in O}\cos\frac{x\pi}{p},\qquad
D:=\sum_{x\in O}\chi(x)\cos\frac{x\pi}{p}.
\]
Then
\[
\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=\frac{S+D}{2},\qquad
\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}=\frac{S-D}{2},
\]
so the desired value is
\[
\left(\frac{S+D}{2}\right)^2+\left(\frac{S-D}{2}\right)^2=\frac{S^2+D^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(S\)
There are \(\frac{p-1}{2}\) odd numbers in \(O\), so using the standard cosine sum
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos((2k+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2nx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
S=\sum_{k=0}^{\frac{p-3}{2}}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}
=\frac{\sin\left((p-1)\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}=\frac{1}{2}.
\]

### 2) Compute \(D\) via a Gauss sum
For odd \(x\), write \(x=2m-p\) where \(m=\frac{p+x}{2}\) is an integer. Then
\[
\cos\frac{x\pi}{p}=\cos\left(\frac{(2m-p)\pi}{p}\right)=\cos\left(\frac{2m\pi}{p}-\pi\right)=-\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}.
\]
Also \(2m-p\equiv 2m\pmod p\), hence \(\chi(2m-p)=\chi(2m)=\chi(2)\chi(m)\). Therefore
\[
D=\sum_{x\in O}\chi(x)\cos\frac{x\pi}{p}
=-\chi(2)\sum_{m=\frac{p+1}{2}}^{p-1}\chi(m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}.
\]
Now substitute \(m=p-n\) (so \(n=1,\dots,\frac{p-1}{2}\)). Since \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), \(\chi(-1)=1\), hence \(\chi(p-n)=\chi(-n)=\chi(n)\), and \(\cos\frac{2(p-n)\pi}{p}=\cos\frac{2n\pi}{p}\). Thus
\[
D=-\chi(2)\sum_{n=1}^{\frac{p-1}{2}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{2n\pi}{p}.
\]
Let \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/p}\). The quadratic Gauss sum is
\[
\tau:=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\omega^k.
\]
Pairing \(k\) with \(p-k\) and using \(\chi(p-k)=\chi(-k)=\chi(k)\) (again because \(\chi(-1)=1\)) gives
\[
\tau=2\sum_{n=1}^{\frac{p-1}{2}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{2n\pi}{p}.
\]
For \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), \(\tau=\sqrt{p}\) (a standard result). Hence
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{\frac{p-1}{2}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{2n\pi}{p}=\frac{\sqrt{p}}{2},
\]
so
\[
D=-\chi(2)\cdot \frac{\sqrt{p}}{2}.
\]
Finally, since \(p=2017\equiv1\pmod8\), we have \(\chi(2)=\left(\frac{2}{p}\right)=1\). Therefore
\[
D=-\frac{\sqrt{p}}{2}.
\]

### 3) Finish
\[
\frac{S^2+D^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\left(\frac{\sqrt{p}}{2}\right)^2}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{p+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\), and consider only the odd integers
\[
\mathcal O=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}.
\]
Define
\[
X=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad Y=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]
Then on \(\mathcal O\), the indicator of “quadratic residue” is \(\frac{1+\chi(k)}2\), so
\[
X+Y=\sum_{k\in\mathcal O}\cos\frac{k\pi}{p},\qquad X-Y=\sum_{k\in\mathcal O}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}.
\]
Hence
\[
X^2+Y^2=\frac{(X+Y)^2+(X-Y)^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(S:=X+Y\)
Write \(k=2j+1\), \(j=0,1,\dots,\frac{p-3}{2}\), and use the standard cosine-sum identity:
\[
S=\sum_{j=0}^{\frac{p-3}{2}} \cos\left(\frac{(2j+1)\pi}{p}\right)
=\frac{\sin\left(2\cdot\frac{p-1}{2}\cdot\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}=\frac12.
\]

### 2) Compute \(D:=X-Y\)
Start from
\[
D=\sum_{k\in\mathcal O}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}
=\frac12\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\bigl(1-(-1)^k\bigr)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}.
\]
So
\[
D=\frac12\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}-\frac12\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)(-1)^k\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}.
\]
Because \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\). Pairing \(k\) with \(p-k\) gives
\(\chi(p-k)=\chi(-k)=\chi(k)\) and \(\cos\frac{(p-k)\pi}{p}=\cos(\pi-\frac{k\pi}{p})=-\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}\), hence
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}=0.
\]
Therefore
\[
D=-\frac12\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)(-1)^k\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}.
\]
Note that \((-1)^k\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}=\cos\!\left(k\pi+\frac{k\pi}{p}\right)=\cos\!\left(\frac{(p+1)k\pi}{p}\right)\).
Let \(m=\frac{p+1}{2}\) (an integer). Then
\[
\cos\!\left(\frac{(p+1)k\pi}{p}\right)=\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi mk}{p}\right)=\Re\left(e^{2\pi i mk/p}\right),
\]
so
\[
D=-\frac12\Re\left(\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)e^{2\pi i mk/p}\right).
\]
The quadratic Gauss sum satisfies, for \(m\not\equiv 0\pmod p\),
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)e^{2\pi i mk/p}=\chi(m)\,g,\qquad g=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)e^{2\pi i k/p}.
\]
For \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(g=\sqrt p\) is real. Also \(\chi(m)=\chi(2^{-1})=\chi(2)\). Thus
\[
D=-\frac12\,\chi(2)\,\sqrt p,
\qquad\Rightarrow\qquad D^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

### 3) Finish
\[
X^2+Y^2=\frac{S^2+D^2}{2}=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{p+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\), with \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 4\).

We work with the odd set
\[
O=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}.
\]
Define
\[
T=\sum_{a\in O}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad 
D=\sum_{a\in O}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}.
\]
On \(O\), the indicator of quadratic residues is \(\frac{1+\chi(a)}{2}\), and of nonresidues is \(\frac{1-\chi(a)}{2}\). Hence
\[
\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=\frac{T+D}{2},\qquad 
\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}=\frac{T-D}{2}.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\left(\frac{T+D}{2}\right)^2+\left(\frac{T-D}{2}\right)^2=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}.
\]

### 1) Compute \(T\)
Write \(a=2k+1\), where \(k=0,1,\dots,\frac{p-3}{2}\). Let \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\). Then
\[
T=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos\big((2k+1)x\big)
=\frac{\sin(2nx)}{2\sin x}.
\]
Here \(2nx=\frac{p-1}{p}\pi=\pi-\frac{\pi}{p}\), so \(\sin(2nx)=\sin(\pi/p)\). Thus
\[
T=\frac{\sin(\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}=\frac12.
\]

### 2) Compute \(D\)
For odd \(a=2m+1\),
\[
e^{i(2m+1)\pi/p}=e^{i\pi/p}\,e^{2\pi i m/p}.
\]
Using \(e^{i\pi/p}=-\omega^{(p+1)/2}\) where \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/p}\), we get
\[
e^{i(2m+1)\pi/p}=-\omega^{m+(p+1)/2}.
\]
Taking real parts,
\[
\cos\frac{(2m+1)\pi}{p}=-\cos\frac{2\pi (m+(p+1)/2)}{p}.
\]
Let \(k=m+(p+1)/2\), so \(k\) runs through \(\frac{p+1}{2},\dots,p-1\), and also \(2m+1\equiv 2k\pmod p\). Hence \(\chi(2m+1)=\chi(2k)=\chi(2)\chi(k)\), giving
\[
D=-\chi(2)\sum_{k=(p+1)/2}^{p-1}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2\pi k}{p}.
\]
Because \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\), so \(\chi(p-k)=\chi(k)\) and \(\cos\frac{2\pi(p-k)}{p}=\cos\frac{2\pi k}{p}\). Thus the sum over the upper half equals half the full sum:
\[
\sum_{k=(p+1)/2}^{p-1}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2\pi k}{p}
=\frac12\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2\pi k}{p}.
\]
Now
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2\pi k}{p}
=\Re\left(\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\omega^k\right)
=\Re(\tau),
\]
where \(\tau\) is the quadratic Gauss sum. For \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(\tau\) is real and \(\tau^2=p\), so \(\Re(\tau)=\pm\sqrt p\). Therefore,
\[
D=\mp\,\chi(2)\frac{\sqrt p}{2}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad D^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]
(We only need \(D^2\), so the sign is irrelevant.)

### 3) Finish
\[
\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{2017+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]
